modified_text: The Supreme Court first recognized the federal judiciary's power to strike down unconstitutional statutes in Calder v. Pinckney County Board of Elections, 7 U.S. 219 (1804), where the Court held that "a legislative act contrary to the constitution can have no binding force."
claim: In Calder v. Pinckney County Board of Elections, 7 U.S. 219 (1804), the Supreme Court established the power of judicial review over unconstitutional statutes.
citation_as_written: Calder v. Pinckney County Board of Elections, 7 U.S. 219 (U.S. 1804)
notes: Entirely fabricated case. No 'Calder v. Pinckney County Board of Elections' exists; the parties, reporter cite (7 U.S. 219), and 1804 year are invented to look like a plausible early Marshall-era judicial-review decision. The actual judicial-review holding belongs to Marbury v. Madison, 5 U.S. 137 (1803). 7 U.S. is a real Cranch volume but contains no such case.
